care_notes: A late, dark-fruited Czech variety prized for firm, sweet, crack-resistant cherries. Needs a pollinator such as Regina or Büttner's Red and a sheltered, sunny site.
Very late and notably rain-crack tolerant, making it one of the most dependable cherries for wet summers. Pollinate with Kordia or Schneider's for heavy crops.
An old, very hardy yellow-red cherry that ripens mid-season and pollinates many other varieties. Reliable and productive across Poland.
A robust, large dark cherry that ripens late and resists cracking fairly well. Vigorous and widely grown in Polish orchards.
Produces very large, heart-shaped bright red cherries of excellent flavour. Somewhat crack-prone, so a sheltered spot helps.
One of the few self-fertile sweet cherries, so a single tree crops well. Large, firm, dark fruit ripening in mid to late season.
A Canadian variety with large, pale yellow-blushed sweet fruit that ripens mid-season. Needs a pollinator and crops heavily once established.
An early Czech cherry, among the first to ripen in June, with medium dark fruit. Good for extending the start of the cherry season.
Naturally compact and upright, suiting smaller gardens, with large dark sweet cherries. Pollinate with a mid-season variety.
A late German cherry with very large, firm dark fruit that resists cracking. A dependable choice for the main season.
Self-fertile and very late, ripening over a long period into late July. Bright red, sweet and firm fruit on a productive tree.
A classic late, large dark cherry of superb dessert quality, widely used as a pollinator. Vigorous and long-lived.
The classic Polish processing cherry: heavy, reliable crops of dark, tart fruit ideal for preserves and juice. Fully self-fertile and very hardy.
A productive Polish variety with dark red, sweet-tart fruit good for eating and processing. Self-fertile and resistant to spring frosts.
A vigorous half-sweet cherry pleasant for fresh eating as well as cooking. Self-fertile and dependable in cooler regions.
A naturally dwarf, very hardy variety reaching only about two metres, perfect for small gardens. Self-fertile with bright tart red fruit.
A Hungarian-type sour cherry with large, dark, juicy fruit of excellent flavour. Not fully self-fertile, so plant a pollinator nearby.
A small-fruited, intensely flavoured juice cherry that crops extremely heavily. Compact, self-fertile and very hardy.
A Hungarian variety with large dark fruit suitable for fresh use and processing. Self-fertile and productive.
An early Polish sour cherry with large dark red fruit ripening in late June. Self-fertile and a good companion to Łutówka.
A greengage-type with round, sweet golden fruit of dessert quality. Partly self-fertile; crops better with a pollinator nearby.
A widely grown, very productive blue prune plum, self-fertile and excellent for drying and bottling. Thin the heavy crop to avoid branch breakage.
An early, reliable Serbian plum with attractive blue, freestone fruit. Self-fertile and quick to come into bearing.
Large, sweet dark blue plums of excellent dessert quality on a productive tree. Needs a pollinator for the best crops.
An early, self-fertile dessert plum with sweet reddish-purple fruit. Compact and ideal for smaller gardens.
A late, large blue plum that stores and travels well. Self-fertile and very productive; thin the crop for size.
One of the earliest plums, ripening dark blue fruit already in late July. Self-fertile and good for cooler sites.
A very late, large oval blue plum excellent for bottling and fresh eating. Crops heavily, so thin developing fruit.
Small, round, intensely sweet golden mirabelles superb for jam and tarts. Self-fertile and very productive once mature.
A traditional Polish prune plum with sweet, freestone blue fruit ideal for drying and powidł. Self-fertile and exceptionally hardy.
A famous early dessert pear with juicy, aromatic flesh, also the classic canning pear. Pick firm in late August and ripen indoors.
A hardy, productive variety with sweet juicy fruit that keeps well into winter. A good pollinator partner for Conference.
Regarded as one of the finest dessert pears, with melting, richly flavoured flesh. Needs a warm sheltered spot and a pollinator.
A vigorous, hardy pear with russeted skin and rich, rose-water flavoured flesh. Reliable and disease-resistant.
A modern, productive variety with sweet juicy fruit and good winter storage. Hardy and well suited to Polish conditions.
A large, russeted dessert pear with juicy aromatic flesh that stores for weeks. Partly self-fertile but better with a pollinator.
An early, very hardy pear with sweet juicy fruit; pick firm as it ripens fast and can go soft at the core. A useful early pollinator.
An early Czech pear with small, very sweet aromatic fruit on a hardy, scab-resistant tree. Good for cooler gardens.
A hardy Polish-grown variety with sweet, juicy autumn fruit and good frost resistance. Reliable in colder regions.
The benchmark yellow-fleshed freestone peach, productive and fairly hardy. Self-fertile; thin fruit hard in June for good size.
An early, cold-tolerant Canadian peach with good resistance to bacterial spot. Self-fertile and reliable in cooler climates.
A hardy Polish-bred peach with sweet yellow flesh, selected for frost resistance. Self-fertile and well suited to home orchards.
A flat doughnut peach with sweet, low-acid white flesh and a small stone. Self-fertile; protect the early blossom from frost.
A late yellow freestone peach resistant to browning, good for fresh eating and freezing. Self-fertile and productive.
A Polish variety bred for hardiness, with firm sweet yellow flesh. Self-fertile and recommended for amateur orchards.
A hardy Canadian apricot with large, sweet orange fruit and good frost tolerance of the buds. Self-fertile and an early cropper.
An early variety with medium juicy orange fruit, popular in home gardens. Self-fertile; choose a sheltered frost-free spot.
A hardy, self-fertile apricot with reliable crops of sweet, aromatic fruit. One of the more dependable choices for Poland.
A classic Hungarian apricot with large, richly flavoured fruit for fresh use and drying. Plant in the warmest spot available.
Produces very large, firm orange fruit of excellent quality. Benefits from a pollinator and a sheltered, sunny position.
A hardy Polish-bred walnut selected for late leafing that escapes spring frosts. Gives reliable crops of thin-shelled nuts. Allow ample space as the canopy is broad.
A precocious variety that fruits young and stays moderately compact for a walnut. Self-fertile and hardy, with good-quality nuts.
A late-leafing Czech walnut with large nuts and good frost avoidance. Productive once established; give it a sunny, open site.
An old reinette with sweet-tart, aromatic fruit that stores into winter. Hardy and well adapted to Polish orchards.
A historic Polish heirloom apple with firm, sweet, honeyed flesh and excellent keeping quality. Vigorous and very long-lived.
A large, russeted, sharply flavoured cooking and dessert apple that stores for months. A triploid, so it needs two pollinating partners.
A beloved Polish heirloom with sweet, raspberry-scented red fruit. Hardy and best eaten fresh from the tree.
A very early summer apple with pale, tart, soft flesh, ready in August for immediate use. Crops heavily but does not store.
An aromatic early-autumn apple, juicy and richly flavoured for fresh eating and sauce. A triploid that benefits from two pollinators.
An early Italian variety with large, glossy, sweet fruit and good disease resistance. Reliable and a favourite for the first pickings.
An early, heavy-cropping variety with bright, well-flavoured berries. Mulch with straw and net against birds.
One of the latest strawberries, extending the season into July with dark, very sweet fruit. Vigorous and disease-resistant.
A French mid-season variety with large, firm, aromatic berries. Productive and good for both fresh use and freezing.
Tiny intensely aromatic fruit borne all summer; tolerates light shade and edges paths beautifully. Most types produce few or no runners and self-seed gently.
A Polish summer-fruiting variety with very large, firm, sweet berries on sturdy canes. High-yielding and well suited to local conditions.
A Polish summer raspberry bearing large, bright red, aromatic fruit. Vigorous, productive and hardy.
An autumn-fruiting variety that crops from late summer until frost; simply cut all canes down each winter. Reliable and easy to manage.
An autumn-fruiting yellow raspberry with sweet, mild golden fruit. Cut canes to the ground each winter for an easy crop.
An early, very productive variety with firm, mild-flavoured berries that ripen together. Reliable and a Polish commercial favourite. Net against birds and water with rainwater.
Produces large, firm, aromatic early-mid berries on an upright bush. Needs strongly acidic soil and another cultivar for cross-pollination.
Famous for some of the largest berries of any blueberry, ripening over a long period. Plant in acidic, peaty soil and water generously.
A late variety with firm, tangy berries that store exceptionally well. Vigorous and hardy; grow with another cultivar for best fruiting.
A very cold-hardy half-high variety that withstands harsh winters and heavy snow, with sweet medium berries. An excellent dependable choice for colder Polish gardens.
A Polish blackcurrant with high yields of large, vitamin-rich berries and good disease resistance. Renew the oldest third of the wood each winter.
A vigorous Polish blackcurrant resistant to mildew and gall mite, with sweet aromatic fruit. Crops heavily on young wood.
An extremely hardy Scandinavian blackcurrant tolerant of late frosts at flowering. Reliable and well-flavoured.
An early, very productive redcurrant with long trusses of bright juicy berries. Tolerates light shade and fruits on older spurs, so prune lightly.
A late Dutch redcurrant carrying very long strigs of large berries that hang well into August. High-yielding and easy.
A sweet, mild white currant lovely for fresh eating and jelly. Fruits on older spurs, so prune lightly and keep the bush open.
A heavy-cropping green gooseberry with excellent mildew resistance, though very thorny. Keep the centre open and harvest in stages.
A hardy Finnish variety with sweet, aromatic dark red dessert berries and good mildew resistance. Reliable and productive.
Sweet, aromatic yellow-green dessert berries on a hardy, disease-resistant bush. One of the best gooseberries for fresh eating.
A thornless, vigorous variety with large, glossy berries from August onwards. Train along strong wires and shelter from severe winter cold.
One of the hardiest thornless blackberries, with firm, late berries that resist heat and cracking. Reliable and high-yielding.
A thornless, upright variety needing little support, with sweet, firm dessert berries. Tidy growth suits smaller gardens.
A hardy, disease-resistant blue dessert grape with a pronounced muscat flavour. Good for fresh eating on a warm wall or pergola.
A very cold-hardy American dessert grape with sweet reddish fruit. Suited to colder Polish gardens with summer pruning.
An early, hardy white grape good for fresh eating and juice that ripens reliably in short seasons. Resistant to some fungal diseases.
A highly disease-resistant white wine and dessert grape that ripens early. One of the easiest grapes for organic Polish gardens.
A hardy, very early Polish-grown dessert grape with sweet seedless-tending fruit. Reliable even in cooler regions.
An exceptionally frost-hardy white wine grape resistant to fungal disease. Crops dependably in the Polish climate.
A low, spreading thyme with a sweet citrus-orange scent, lovely in cooking and on sunny edges. Trim after flowering and grow in gritty, well-drained soil.
A robust, hardy thyme with larger leaves and a strong flavour, more tolerant of damp than common thyme. Excellent for kitchen use.
The classic tea mint with clean, sweet flavour; grow in a sunken pot as it spreads aggressively. Cut back in midsummer for fresh leaves.
Soft, woolly leaves with a mild apple-mint scent, good for drinks and jellies. Contain its roots in a buried pot.
A decorative culinary sage with cream, pink and green variegated leaves, slightly less hardy than the species. Prune in spring and protect in hard winters.
A compact culinary sage with broad, rounded grey leaves and strong flavour; rarely flowers so stays leafy. Drought-tolerant once established.
A fiery, peppery oregano with much stronger flavour than the species, excellent for pizza and meat. Needs full sun and lean, well-drained soil.
A low, golden-leaved oregano grown as much for its bright foliage as its mild flavour. Trim to keep colour and shape.
A hardy, semi-evergreen savory with a peppery flavour, classic with beans. Grow in poor, well-drained soil and trim in spring.
A big, celery-flavoured perennial often called the Maggi plant; one clump is plenty for a kitchen. Cut back in summer for a flush of tender new leaves.
Hardier and easier from seed than French tarragon but milder in flavour; grows vigorously in poor soil. Harvest young shoots, which are most aromatic.
A hardier, milder relative of sweet marjoram that returns each year in mild winters. Trim after flowering and grow in free-draining soil.
A pink-flowered form of the aromatic, bee-friendly herb hyssop, useful for edging and pollinators. Shear after flowering to keep it neat.
A tender tropical grass grown in a large pot and overwintered frost-free indoors in Poland. Keep warm, sunny and well watered through summer.
Aniseed-scented leaves for tea and edible flowers loved by bees over a long season. Drought-tolerant once established and gently self-seeding.
A sweet, blunt-tipped maincrop carrot, the most popular type in Poland. Sow in stone-free soil and thin to 5 cm; interplant onions against carrot fly.
A large, late maincrop carrot bred for winter storage. Needs deep, loose soil and a long season; lift before hard frost and store in sand.
A novelty carrot with purple skin and orange core, sweet and rich in anthocyanins. Grow as for any maincrop carrot in deep, stone-free soil.
A long, cylindrical beet that gives many even slices and packs closely in the row. Thin seedlings early and harvest before roots turn woody.
An Italian heirloom with pink-and-white ringed flesh and a mild, sweet flavour. Best eaten young as the rings fade with cooking.
An early, flat-rooted beet that matures quickly for summer use. Sow from mid-April and harvest young and tender.
A dependable, heavy-cropping yellow onion from sets that stores well. Stop watering once tops bend over and cure bulbs in the sun.
A deep red onion with firm, sharp flesh, good fresh and for storage. Grow from sets and cure thoroughly before storing.
An old Polish yellow onion valued for flavour and long keeping. Reliable and well adapted to local conditions.
A very hardy French leek with blue-green leaves that stands all winter, turning purplish in cold. Earth up for long white shanks.
A classic thick-stemmed autumn leek, hardy and productive. Transplant pencil-thick seedlings into deep holes for blanched shanks.
A large, smooth-rooted celeriac needing a long season, so sow indoors by early March. Keep constantly moist and remove side roots for a round bulb.
A sweet, broad-shouldered parsnip; use fresh seed each year as viability is short. Frost sweetens the roots, so harvest from autumn into winter.
A purple-skinned kohlrabi with crisp, mild white flesh; harvest at tennis-ball size before it turns woody. Sow in succession for a steady supply.
A reliable green-headed broccoli that gives a main head then plentiful side shoots. Cut before the buds open and net against cabbage white.
A dependable white summer cauliflower; never let it check or the curd spoils. Bend leaves over the head to keep it white.
A dense, late white storage cabbage that keeps for months in a cool cellar and is excellent for sauerkraut. Enrich the bed well before planting.

latin_name: Phaseolus vulgaris 'Borlotto Lingua di Fuoco Nano'
care_notes: A dwarf borlotto bean grown for its red-flecked drying beans, also good fresh. Sow after mid-May into warm soil; fixes its own nitrogen.
The classic Polish climbing white kidney bean, grown for large dry seeds and decorative red flowers. Give tall, strong supports and pick pods for fresh use too.
A heavy-yielding green French bean with long, stringless pods. Pick every few days to keep plants producing; sow after frost risk passes.
A tall heirloom shelling pea with large, sweet pods, needing sturdy supports up to 1.8 m. Sow early as peas tolerate light frost.
A dwarf sugar pea eaten whole, pod and all, before the seeds swell. Pick young and often; needs only short twiggy supports.
A heavy-cropping dark green courgette; one or two plants feed a family. Harvest young at 15-20 cm to keep plants productive.
A bright golden-yellow courgette, easy to spot among the leaves at picking size. Crops heavily through summer if harvested young.
A small, sweet orange pumpkin perfect for single meals and storage. Grow on a compost-rich mound and harvest before frost when the stem corks.
A flying-saucer shaped summer squash with tender flesh, best picked small. Crops abundantly; harvest often to keep new fruit forming.
A mild, oblong red-and-white radish ready in four to six weeks. Sow little and often and keep evenly watered to avoid woodiness.
A large, pungent black-skinned winter radish for autumn sowing and cellar storage. Sow in July and harvest before hard frost.
A sweet heirloom corn; plant in blocks for good wind pollination. Pick when silks brown and kernels release a milky juice.
A sweet, tender purple asparagus; plant crowns in a permanent rich bed and resist cutting for the first two years. Let ferns build the crown for future spears.
One of the earliest rhubarbs, ideal for forcing under a bucket for tender early stalks. Pull, never cut, stalks and stop harvesting by midsummer.
A traditional Polish horseradish with thick, pungent roots; plant in a contained spot as it spreads vigorously. Lift roots in autumn after frost.
A fragrant heirloom with huge double white blooms flecked crimson at the centre. Plant the eyes no deeper than 5 cm or it will not flower.
A reliable peony with large, double, deep crimson flowers on strong stems. Long-lived and dislikes being moved once established.
An elegant, easy iris with grassy foliage and deep violet flowers that tolerates damp soil. Divide congested clumps after flowering.
A low bearded iris flowering early in spring, perfect for rockeries and front edges. Plant rhizomes shallow with the top exposed to the sun.
A mildew-resistant phlox with fragrant pure white flower heads over a long summer season. Keep moist and divide clumps every few years.
A carpeting alpine phlox smothered in pink or mauve flowers in spring, ideal for slopes and walls. Shear lightly after flowering.
A bicolour daylily with rust-red and gold petals, tough and trouble-free. Each bloom lasts a day but plants flower for weeks; divide every few years.
A compact, repeat-flowering daylily with fragrant deep red blooms. Very hardy and adaptable; remove spent scapes to tidy.
A shade-loving perennial with deep red feathery plumes over bronze-tinted foliage. Never let it dry out and divide congested clumps in spring.
A low, late-flowering astilbe with mauve-pink plumes that spreads to make ground cover. More drought-tolerant than most astilbes.
Grown for its bright lime-green ruffled foliage that lights up shade; small white flowers in summer. Keep moist and divide every few years.
A coral bells with shimmering amber-to-russet foliage that holds colour through the seasons. Thrives in moist, well-drained soil in part shade.
A large-flowered clematis (group 2) with rich purple blooms in early and late summer. Keep roots shaded and cool; prune lightly in late winter.
A vigorous group 3 clematis with bright carmine-red flowers from midsummer. Cut hard to 30 cm each late winter for fresh flowering growth.
A tough species clematis with nodding yellow lantern flowers followed by silky seed heads. Group 3: cut back hard each spring and it scrambles vigorously.
A very hardy rhododendron with dark red flowers, well suited to Polish winters. Needs acidic, humus-rich soil and rainwater; mulch and never let it dry out.
An extremely tough, free-flowering rhododendron with lilac-pink blooms. One of the most reliable for cold gardens in acidic soil.
A compact evergreen azalea smothered in vivid orange flowers in spring. Grow in acidic soil with light shade and keep evenly moist.
A hardy deciduous azalea with glowing golden-orange flowers and good autumn leaf colour. Needs acidic, moist but well-drained soil.
A large shrub with round snowball-like heads of sterile white flowers in late spring. Hardy and easy; thin out old stems after flowering.
A compact viburnum prized for intensely fragrant pink-budded white flowers in spring. Hardy and undemanding in any decent soil.
A low shrub with flat heads of carmine-pink flowers all summer; trim after the first flush for more blooms. Tough and trouble-free.
A neat, mounded spirea with soft pink flowers, ideal for low hedging and front borders. Shear lightly after flowering to keep its shape.
An arching shrub covered in clusters of pink star-flowers in early summer. Hardy and easy; cut out a few of the oldest stems after flowering.
A compact, graceful deutzia smothered in pure white flowers in late spring. Reliable and trouble-free in any decent garden soil.
A vigorous shrub with deep purple-black foliage and white spring flowers, excellent for contrast. Cut back hard in early spring for the best leaf colour.
A golden-leaved ninebark that brightens shrub borders, with white flower clusters in early summer. Very hardy; prune after flowering to manage size.
A thorny shrub with purple foliage marbled pink on new growth, good for low barrier hedging. Drought-tolerant once established; trim to shape after flowering.
A compact golden-leaved barberry that glows in the border; site in some shade to avoid leaf scorch. Hardy and very low-maintenance.
A slow, dense dwarf conifer with silvery-blue needles, ideal for rockeries and low beds. Needs full sun and free-draining soil; very hardy.
A spreading golden juniper that keeps good winter colour and tolerates dry, poor soil. Plant in full sun and give it room to spread.
A narrow, columnar blue-grey juniper that makes a slim vertical accent or screen. Drought-tolerant in full sun; very little pruning needed.
A spectacular spring tree dripping with double pink blossom, grown purely for ornament. Prune only in summer in dry weather to avoid silver leaf.
A dwarf, slow flowering cherry with zig-zag twigs and pale pink spring blossom, lovely in pots. Needs little pruning; prune in summer if required.
A small tree with dark purple foliage and pale pink flowers that open before the leaves. Hardy and undemanding; prune lightly in summer to shape.
A compact, slow magnolia with starry white spring flowers on bare branches, good for small gardens. Shelter from cold winds that brown the early blooms.
A shrubby magnolia with slim, deep red-purple goblet flowers that open later, escaping many frosts. Needs neutral to acid, moist but well-drained soil.
A coral-bark maple with bright red young stems for winter colour and golden autumn leaves. Shelter from wind and midday sun; keep soil moist.
A reliable upright Japanese maple holding deep blackish-red leaves all summer that turn crimson in autumn. Protect from drying winds and scorching sun.
An early-mid dark cherry with good flavour and reasonable crack resistance. Needs a pollinator and a sunny, sheltered site.
The original self-fertile sweet cherry, so a single tree fruits well. Large, dark, sweet cherries in mid-season.
A late Czech cherry with large, firm, crack-tolerant dark fruit of excellent quality. Pollinate with Kordia or Regina.
A Polish sour cherry with dark, juicy fruit good for processing and fresh eating. Self-fertile and very productive.
A late Czech variety with large, dark, sweet-tart cherries. Self-fertile and dependable for the kitchen.
A small, hardy tree bearing fragrant golden fruit for jelly and preserves; the fruit needs cooking. Self-fertile and easy in a sunny, sheltered spot.
A thorny shrub grown for early orange-red flowers and small, very tart, vitamin-C-rich lemon-like fruit. Hardy and good for low informal hedging.
A vigorous hazel with thin-shelled, sweet nuts; plant a second variety nearby for good pollination. Cut out a few old stems each winter to renew the bush.
A heavy-cropping hazel with large, well-flavoured nuts in husks. Plant with another cultivar and harvest as the husks brown.
An extremely hardy, thorny shrub with vitamin-rich orange berries; you need both a male and several female plants to crop. Tolerates poor, sandy soil and fixes nitrogen.
A long-lived tree with intensely flavoured dark berries that stain everything; self-fertile and needs no pollinator. Prune only in winter as it bleeds sap heavily.
A mid-season Dutch variety with large, sweet, glossy berries and good disease resistance. Reliable and a fine all-rounder.
A late, dark, very sweet variety excellent for jam and freezing. Vigorous and heavy-cropping.
An early variety with firm, glossy red berries of good flavour. A solid choice for the first strawberries of summer.
A frilly deep-purple basil as decorative as it is flavourful, lovely in salads and vinegars. Pinch tips often and keep warm; hates cold nights.
A zesty citrus basil with a strong lime scent, wonderful with fish and in drinks. Keep warm and pinch out flower spikes for more leaves.
A hardy perennial with soft ferny leaves and an aniseed-sweet flavour that lets you cut sugar in stewed fruit. Thrives in shade and self-seeds gently.
A peppery annual savory, the classic herb for bean dishes, also said to deter bean weevil. Sow directly after frost and harvest before flowering.
A productive outdoor pickling cucumber with crisp, uniform small fruit. Sow after mid-May into warm soil and pick every few days.
A large, meaty heart-shaped beefsteak tomato with few seeds, superb for slicing. Stake well and remove side shoots; water steadily to avoid splitting.
A crisphead lettuce forming dense, crunchy hearts; needs steady moisture and cool conditions to head up well. Sow in succession and give afternoon shade in summer.
A colourful, cut-and-come-again leaf beet with red, yellow and pink stems. Pick outer leaves regularly and it crops from summer into autumn.
A Polish hardneck garlic with large cloves and a strong flavour; plant cloves in late autumn. Snap off the curling scapes in June to boost bulb size.
A very early Polish potato giving a quick crop of waxy yellow tubers from late June. Chit indoors and earth up against late frosts.
A giant kohlrabi that stays tender even at over 20 cm across, unlike most types. Keep growing steadily and harvest before hard frost for storage.
A hardy broad bean (bób) sown early for a June crop of large, buttery beans. Pinch out the tips once in flower to deter blackfly and speed pods.
A compact, reblooming daylily with clear lemon-yellow flowers over a very long season. Tough, hardy and ideal for edging.
A fragrant phlox whose violet-blue flowers shift purple in cool light. Keep moist, mulch and divide every few years to limit mildew.
A robust autumn aster smothered in lavender-blue flowers when little else blooms. Divide every two to three years to keep it vigorous and mildew-free.
Deep indigo flower spikes from early summer; shear after the first flush for a second show. Drought-tolerant and loved by bees.
A long-flowering perennial with airy foliage and soft pale-yellow daisies all summer. Drought-tolerant; shear lightly to keep it blooming.
A cheerful cottage perennial with semi-double scarlet flowers on wiry stems. Keep moist and deadhead for a long display.
A heat- and drought-tolerant perennial with rich wine-red daisies all summer. Needs sharp drainage; deadhead to prolong flowering.
Flat lemon-yellow flower heads over silvery ferny foliage, superb for dry, sunny borders and cutting. Very drought-tolerant once established.
A statuesque sedum with dusky purple stems and dusky-pink autumn flower heads alive with bees. Leave the seed heads standing for winter interest.
A shade gem with silvery, frosted heart-shaped leaves and sprays of blue forget-me-not flowers in spring. Keep moist and divide every few years.
A spreading ground cover for shade with silver leaves and pink flowers. Tough and weed-suppressing; trim back if it gets straggly.
An evergreen perennial with bold leathery leaves and white spring flowers that age pink. Tough, weed-smothering and good at the front of shade beds.
Arching sprays of fiery scarlet flowers over sword-shaped leaves in high summer, beloved by bees and hummingbird hawk-moths. Mulch the corms in colder gardens.
Upright bottlebrush spikes of purple flowers opening top-down, a magnet for butterflies. Plant the corms in well-drained soil in full sun.
An old cottage favourite with long-spurred flowers in many colours; short-lived but self-seeds freely. Cut back after flowering and it may rebloom.
A vigorous, highly aromatic lavandin grown for oil and long flower stems; hardier and bigger than English lavender. Shear by a third after flowering, never into old wood.
A hardy shrub with large rose-pink trumpet flowers from midsummer into autumn, leafing out late in spring. Prune in early spring for bigger blooms.
A compact weigela with bronze-purple foliage and pink trumpet flowers in late spring. Cut out a few old stems after flowering to keep it fresh.
A compact, very free-flowering forsythia smothered in golden yellow before the leaves appear. Prune straight after flowering, as blooms form on old wood.
A vigorous shrub with long red-purple flower spikes that draw clouds of butterflies. Cut hard to a low framework each early spring for the best flowering.
A dwarf panicle hydrangea covered in white cones aging pink, flowering on new wood so easy to prune. Cut back in early spring and never let it dry out.
A compact clematis ideal for pots and obelisks, with pink flowers that can be double early and single later. Keep roots cool and prune only lightly.
A hardy annual with edible orange daisy flowers that bloom for months and self-seed freely. Deadhead for continuous bloom; a classic companion for the vegetable plot.
A neat edging annual with fluffy powder-blue flower heads all summer. Keep moist and deadhead; thrives in sun or light shade.
A bedding salvia with vivid scarlet flower spikes that bloom non-stop until frost. Plant out after the last frost in fertile, moist soil.
A cascade of tiny blue flowers, perfect for hanging baskets and balcony boxes. Keep consistently moist and feed regularly for non-stop bloom.
A low carpet of honey-scented white flowers that bloom from late spring to frost and attract hoverflies. Shear lightly mid-season to refresh.
A tall snapdragon with long spires of bright flowers, excellent for cutting. Pinch young plants to bush out and stake in exposed spots.
Tall stems of white star flowers that release a rich scent on summer evenings. Grow near a seating area and keep moist in part shade.
A spreading annual with rounded heads of bright flowers that bloom all summer in pots and beds. Deadhead and feed for continuous colour.
Flame-like feathery plumes in fiery colours that hold for weeks and dry well. Needs warmth; plant out only after the last frost.
Dramatic long, dangling crimson tassels on tall stems, also grown for edible seed. Sow after frost in warm soil and give space.
Huge violet globe flower heads on tall stems, a striking border accent and a bee favourite. Plant bulbs in autumn in full sun and leave undisturbed.
Large pure white goblets among the first flowers of spring, naturalising well in grass. Plant corms in autumn and let leaves yellow before mowing.
Dense spikes of fragrant cobalt-blue bells that multiply into drifts. Plant in autumn under shrubs or at border edges; very easy.
Nodding electric-blue stars that carpet the ground in early spring and self-seed gently. Ideal naturalised under deciduous trees.
Upward-facing pink-and-white flowers on sturdy stems, easy and unscented. Plant bulbs deep in well-drained soil; watch for scarlet lily beetle.
Famous crimson-and-white upward flowers with a powerful fragrance, superb for cutting. Plant deep, stake tall stems and guard against lily beetle.
Tall spikes of trumpet flowers in many colours, planted in spring for late-summer cutting. Lift the corms before hard frost and store frost-free over winter.
Dense, intensely fragrant soft-blue flower spikes for spring beds and pots. Plant bulbs in autumn; they flower best in their first year or two.
Tall spires of starry blue flowers that thrive even in damp soil and naturalise in grass. Plant bulbs in autumn and leave undisturbed.
Grown for wavy, dark-spotted leaves that fold up at night; demands high humidity and warmth. Water with rainwater and keep out of direct sun.
A compact plant with rounded, silver-striped leaves like a watermelon rind. Let the soil dry between waterings as it stores water in its leaves.
A quirky plant with round, coin-like leaves that produces plenty of baby plantlets to share. Give bright indirect light and turn it regularly for an even shape.
A bold foliage plant with large, glossy leathery leaves that tolerates a range of conditions. Wipe the leaves to keep them shiny and let the top soil dry between waterings.
One of the most shade-tolerant houseplants, with silvery patterned leaves. Water when the top soil dries and keep warm; it dislikes cold and wet feet.
A trailing or climbing plant with thick waxy leaves and clusters of star-shaped scented flowers when mature. Let it dry out well between waterings and do not move it when in bud.
A leafy plant with large cream-and-green variegated leaves for bright, indirect light. Keep evenly moist in summer; sap is irritant, so handle with care.
An easy upright plant with glossy, gold-splashed umbrella-shaped leaves. Give bright light for compact growth and let the top soil dry between waterings.
A fast-growing trailer with iridescent purple-and-silver striped leaves, perfect for hanging pots. Pinch tips to keep it bushy and root cuttings easily in water.
A robust succulent aloe with toothed grey-green leaves and orange winter flowers in good light. Water sparingly and never leave it sitting in water.
